subsystem: Dovecot Pigeonhole ManageSieve protocol argument parser. This is managesieve_parser_save_arg(), the routine that materialises one parsed protocol argument (atom, quoted string, or literal) into a struct managesieve_arg after the tokenizer has isolated the raw bytes for that argument. It runs inside the ManageSieve command-line parser that decodes the client-supplied wire protocol (RFC 5804).
provenance: Identified by the symbol names and types: managesieve_parser, managesieve_arg, managesieve_arg_create, MANAGESIEVE_ARG_ATOM/STRING/LITERAL, MANAGESIEVE_PARSE_FLAG_*, and the Dovecot-specific allocation/string helpers p_strndup, str_unescape, i_assert, i_unreached, and pool-based allocation (parser->pool). These are Dovecot / Pigeonhole ManageSieve idioms. The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_2324_vuln' and file name train_0290_cwe787.c indicate this is the vulnerable revision and the target class is an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787).
trust_boundary: The (data, size) pair is the raw, still-quoted/escaped byte range of a single argument taken directly off the ManageSieve client connection; a remote, possibly unauthenticated client controls its length, its content (including quotes, backslash escapes, and embedded NUL bytes), and — via the preceding tokenizer state — parser->cur_type, parser->flags, and parser->str_first_escape. Everything reached through data, size, and str_first_escape is hostile. The pool allocations and the managesieve_arg produced here are consumed by downstream command handlers, so any length/offset desync established here propagates into later code.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point that commits one parsed argument. It takes the parser state, a pointer 'data' to the raw bytes of the argument as they appeared on the wire, and 'size', the byte length of that range. 'data' is const unsigned char* — a raw byte buffer, not a NUL-terminated C string — so it may contain embedded NUL bytes, quotes, and backslash escapes. managesieve_arg_create(parser) allocates a fresh struct managesieve_arg from the parser pool and returns it in 'arg'; the switch then fills arg according to the argument type the tokenizer recorded in parser->cur_type.

The atom branch. p_strndup(parser->pool, data, size) copies at most 'size' bytes of 'data' into a newly pool-allocated buffer and appends a terminating NUL, storing the pointer in arg->_data.str. arg->str_len is then set to the raw 'size'. Note the dual bookkeeping: the buffer is a NUL-terminated C string, but the recorded length is the raw byte count 'size'. If 'data' contained an embedded NUL, the C-string view (used by strlen-based consumers) and the str_len view diverge — a length desync established at the trust boundary.

The quoted-string branch. The comment states the invariant that 'data' is quoted (surrounded by '"') and may contain backslash escapes, so 'data' includes the leading quote character and the byte accounting downstream depends on that. When the STRING_STREAM flag is set, the argument is not copied at all: arg is turned into a stream reference (arg->_data.str_stream = parser->str_stream) and no bounds arithmetic occurs. The interesting, dangerous path is the else branch below.

The core string-materialisation path. i_assert(size > 0) is a Dovecot runtime assertion (always compiled in; it aborts, it is not a no-op like C assert) intended to guarantee at least the opening quote is present before the pointer arithmetic. str = p_strndup(parser->pool, data+1, size-1) copies size-1 bytes starting one byte past 'data' — i.e. it strips the leading '"' — into a pool buffer of size (size-1)+1. If unescaping is enabled and an escape was seen (parser->str_first_escape >= 0), str_unescape is invoked at offset str_first_escape-1 into the freshly allocated buffer; the '-1' compensates for having dropped the leading quote from the copy. str_unescape rewrites the string in place, collapsing backslash escapes. Finally arg->_data.str = str and arg->str_len = strlen(str) — here the recorded length is the C-string length (stops at the first NUL), unlike the atom/literal branches that use raw 'size'.

The literal branch ({N+CRLF}...) handles octet-count-prefixed data that is NOT quoted and NOT escaped. If STRING_STREAM is set it becomes a stream reference. Otherwise the raw 'size' bytes are copied verbatim via p_strndup(parser->pool, data, size) and arg->str_len = size; the only difference between the LITERAL_TYPE and default sub-branches is the arg->type tag (MANAGESIEVE_ARG_LITERAL vs MANAGESIEVE_ARG_STRING). Both again record the raw byte length while producing a NUL-terminated buffer, re-establishing the same str_len-vs-C-string desync when the literal payload contains embedded NULs.

The default arm calls i_unreached() (a Dovecot 'this should never happen' abort) to catch a cur_type outside the three handled cases. After the switch, parser->cur_type is reset to ARG_PARSE_NONE so the next argument starts from a clean state. This is normal control-flow hygiene and carries no direct memory-safety contract.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). str was allocated with only size-1 usable bytes (line 26). The only guard on parser->str_first_escape is the lower bound '>= 0'; there is NO upper-bound check that str_first_escape-1 falls inside [0, size-1). str_first_escape is set earlier by the tokenizer from attacker-controlled input as the offset of the first escape within the original quoted token. If that accounting is off by even one — e.g. because an embedded NUL byte in 'data' made the tokenizer's escape offset and the actual copied byte length disagree, or because str_first_escape was computed against the pre-copy buffer that still counted the quote — then str + str_first_escape-1 points at or past the end of the size-1 buffer. str_unescape then scans and rewrites the string in place starting from that bad pointer, walking off the end of the pool allocation: a remote heap out-of-bounds write. This is the central vulnerability of this revision. The implementation must guarantee 0 <= str_first_escape-1 < strlen-of-copied-data before calling str_unescape.

Unsigned integer underflow (CWE-191) latent behind an assertion. size is size_t; if size == 0 then size-1 wraps to SIZE_MAX and p_strndup(parser->pool, data+1, SIZE_MAX) would attempt a massive copy / over-read from data+1. The single defence is i_assert(size > 0). In Dovecot i_assert is always compiled in and aborts, so under normal builds a zero-length quoted string is turned into a controlled crash (denial of service) rather than memory corruption. The risk is twofold: (1) reliance on an assert for memory-safety means any build or refactor that weakens i_assert re-opens a full heap overflow, and (2) an attacker who can drive cur_type==ARG_PARSE_STRING with size==0 gets a remotely triggerable abort/DoS. The implementation must reject or never dispatch a zero-length quoted string on a non-assert path.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
arg->_data.str = p_strndup(parser->pool, data, size);
		arg->str_len = size;
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Length/representation desync from embedded NUL bytes (CWE-170 / feeding CWE-787 downstream) — the essence of CVE-2019-11500. 'data' is raw wire bytes and may contain NUL. p_strndup produces a NUL-terminated C string, but arg->str_len is set to the raw 'size'. For the atom branch (here) and both literal branches (lines 45-46, 49-50) str_len therefore counts bytes past the first embedded NUL, while the STRING branch instead records strlen(str) (line 36), which stops at the first NUL. Consumers that trust arg->str_len will read/copy 'size' bytes from a buffer whose C-string content is shorter, and consumers mixing the two conventions across argument types get inconsistent bounds — the exact class of out-of-bounds heap access Dovecot fixed by rejecting NUL bytes in the parser. The implementation must reject NUL bytes in protocol strings/atoms (or make str_len and the buffer contents consistent) before this point.

Assumed leading-quote offset (CWE-125 / CWE-787 pre-condition). data+1 unconditionally skips one byte on the assumption that 'data' begins with the opening '"'. If the tokenizer ever dispatches cur_type==ARG_PARSE_STRING for a range that is not actually quote-prefixed (protocol edge case, flag interaction, or a future tokenizer change), data+1/size-1 shift the whole copy by one byte, and — critically — the '-1 because we skipped the quote' compensation baked into str_first_escape-1 at line 32 becomes wrong, compounding the out-of-bounds write above. The implementation must uphold the contract that an ARG_PARSE_STRING range always starts with the literal quote byte that size and str_first_escape were computed against.

summary: A remotely reachable ManageSieve argument parser whose quoted-string path performs an unchecked in-place unescape at attacker-influenced offset str_first_escape into a buffer sized size-1, giving a heap out-of-bounds write (CWE-787); a size-1 underflow guarded only by i_assert, and an embedded-NUL length/representation desync (the CVE-2019-11500 class) compound the risk. This is a high-severity memory-corruption surface driven directly by untrusted network input.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: managesieve_parser_save_arg(parser, const unsigned char *data, size_t size)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point taking untrusted (data,size); its size_t size feeds underflow and its bytes feed OOB writes.
Pool allocation of the result struct; no attacker-controlled size, no bound to violate here.
Dispatch on parser state; the default is covered by i_unreached(); risk is in the individual cases.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: ARG_PARSE_ATOM: p_strndup(parser->pool, data, size); arg->str_len = size (lines 14-15)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Raw size stored as length while buffer is NUL-terminated — embedded-NUL length desync (CVE-2019-11500 class).
Sole guard against size-1 underflow; assert-based memory safety is fragile and gives a remote DoS.
size-1 underflows when size==0, and data+1 assumes a leading quote; sets buffer bound for the OOB unescape.
Unbounded offset into a size-1 buffer; in-place unescape is a remote heap out-of-bounds write.
STRING branch records strlen (stops at NUL) while atom/literal record raw size — cross-type length convention mismatch.
Stream path copies nothing and does no size arithmetic; not a memory-safety anchor here.
When set it disables the dangerous str_unescape call; it is a mitigation toggle, not itself a vuln.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: ARG_PARSE_LITERAL_DATA branches: p_strndup(parser->pool, data, size); arg->str_len = size (lines 44-50)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Same raw-size vs NUL-terminated buffer desync as the atom branch for literal payloads.
Only selects the arg->type tag (LITERAL vs STRING); the copy semantics are identical, no distinct contract.
Defensive abort for impossible cur_type; correct hygiene, no security contract.
State reset for the next argument; no memory-safety implication.

findings.title: Heap out-of-bounds write via unchecked str_first_escape offset in in-place unescape
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write (in-place unescape at unbounded offset)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0290_cwe787.c (and str_unescape / tokenizer implementation, files not provided)
findings.invariant: 0 <= parser->str_first_escape-1 and str_first_escape-1 must be strictly less than the length of the copied buffer (size-1), so str + str_first_escape-1 always points inside the pool allocation made at line 26.
findings.failure_mode: The code checks only str_first_escape >= 0, never an upper bound against size-1. str was allocated with size-1 bytes (line 26). If the attacker-influenced escape offset (or the '-1' quote compensation) exceeds the copied length — readily achieved when the tokenizer's escape accounting diverges from the actual copied bytes, e.g. via an embedded NUL or a boundary token — str_unescape begins rewriting the string in place from a pointer at or beyond the buffer end, corrupting adjacent pool heap memory. Remotely triggerable memory corruption / potential RCE.
findings.what_to_confirm: How and where parser->str_first_escape is assigned, and whether any caller-side check guarantees it is within [1, size-1] before this branch; confirm str_unescape's write extent relative to the size-1 allocation.

findings.title: Embedded-NUL length/representation desync between str_len and NUL-terminated buffer
findings.anchor: arg->_data.str = p_strndup(parser->pool, data, size); arg->str_len = size
findings.code:
arg->_data.str = p_strndup(parser->pool, data, size);
		arg->str_len = size;
findings.bug_class: Inconsistent length accounting from embedded NUL (feeds downstream OOB access)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0290_cwe787.c and downstream ManageSieve command handlers (files not provided)
findings.invariant: Protocol atoms/strings/literals must contain no NUL bytes, OR every consumer must treat (str, str_len) consistently so that str_len never claims more valid bytes than the buffer's usable content.
findings.failure_mode: 'data' is raw wire bytes and may include NUL. Here (atom) and in both literal branches (lines 45-46, 49-50) str_len is the raw 'size', counting bytes past an embedded NUL, whereas the quoted-string branch records strlen(str) (line 36), which stops at the first NUL. Consumers that copy str_len bytes from a shorter effective C string, or that mix the two conventions across argument types, perform out-of-bounds heap reads/writes. This is precisely the ManageSieve/IMAP NUL-handling flaw fixed in Dovecot 2.3.7.1 / Pigeonhole 0.5.7.1.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the tokenizer rejects NUL bytes in protocol strings/atoms/literals before save_arg, and whether downstream code trusts arg->str_len or the C-string terminator.

findings.title: Unsigned underflow of size-1 guarded only by i_assert
findings.invariant: size must be >= 1 before computing size-1 on a non-abort path; memory safety must not depend solely on i_assert remaining active.
findings.failure_mode: size is size_t; if size == 0, size-1 wraps to SIZE_MAX and p_strndup(parser->pool, data+1, SIZE_MAX) would over-read/over-copy. i_assert(size > 0) is always compiled in Dovecot and aborts, so today a zero-length quoted string yields a remotely triggerable crash (DoS). If i_assert is ever weakened, disabled, or the branch is refactored, the guard vanishes and a full heap overflow returns.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the tokenizer can never present ARG_PARSE_STRING with size==0, and that i_assert is genuinely fatal (not compiled out) in shipped builds.

findings.title: Fixed +1/-1 quote offset assumes the string range always starts with a quote byte
findings.bug_class: Fixed-offset access on assumed layout (pre-condition for OOB)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0290_cwe787.c and the ManageSieve tokenizer (file not provided)
findings.invariant: An ARG_PARSE_STRING range must always begin with the opening '"' byte, and str_first_escape must have been computed relative to that same quote-prefixed range, so the +1 skip and the -1 compensation at line 32 stay consistent.
findings.failure_mode: data+1 / size-1 unconditionally strip one byte. If a range is dispatched as ARG_PARSE_STRING without the leading quote (protocol/flag edge case or tokenizer change), the copy is shifted by one byte and the '-1 because we skipped the quote' correction at line 32 becomes wrong, directly amplifying the out-of-bounds unescape. This is a latent coupling between the tokenizer and this consumer.
findings.what_to_confirm: That every code path setting cur_type=ARG_PARSE_STRING guarantees data points at the opening quote and str_first_escape is measured from it.

audit_checklist: Trace how parser->str_first_escape is computed and prove str_first_escape-1 is always within [0, size-2) before str_unescape is called (line 32); add an explicit upper-bound reject.
Confirm the tokenizer rejects embedded NUL bytes in atoms, quoted strings, and literals (CVE-2019-11500 fix) so str_len and the NUL-terminated buffer never disagree.
Verify i_assert is fatal in production builds and that ARG_PARSE_STRING with size==0 is impossible; prefer an explicit early return over relying on the assert (lines 23-26).
Check str_unescape's in-place write extent versus the size-1 pool allocation for the worst-case escape density.
Audit every consumer of arg->str_len vs arg->_data.str to ensure a single, consistent length convention across ARG_PARSE_ATOM, ARG_PARSE_STRING, and ARG_PARSE_LITERAL_DATA.
Confirm data always begins with the opening quote when cur_type==ARG_PARSE_STRING so the +1/-1 offset arithmetic holds.
